questions.text: Robin wanted to shove a rubber pipe up a plastic tap. The problem is that the pipe's diameter is smaller than that required to secure a tight fit. So, Robin switched on a hair dryer and pointed it towards the pipe. He found that now the pipe fitted in easily. Why is this so?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The pipe expanded due to the hot air, and then contracted back again
options.explanation: Applying heat from the hair dryer caused the rubber pipe to expand due to the increase in temperature. As the material heated up, its molecules gained energy and moved more vigorously, leading to the expansion of the rubber pipe. This made it more pliable, allowing Robin to easily fit it onto the plastic tap, despite the initial size difference.

options.text: The tap got deformed because of the hot air
options.explanation: The plastic tube expanded because of heat and then when one stopped applying heat, it contracted by cooling. As substances expand on heating and contract when cooled, the pipe fitted in easily. Hot air does not deform the tap.

options.text: The hot air caused adhesion between the rubber and plastic
options.explanation: The hot air from the hair dryer didn't directly cause adhesion between the rubber and plastic. The primary reason the pipe fitted in easily after heating is that the application of heat caused the rubber pipe to expand. This expansion made the rubber more flexible and easier to manipulate, allowing it to stretch and fit onto the plastic tap despite the initial size mismatch.

questions.text: Which is intensive property?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Boiling point is an intensive property because it is characteristic of a substance and does not depend on the amount of the substance present. For example, the boiling point of water at sea level is 100 degrees Celsius, and this value remains constant regardless of the quantity of water.
Molarity is intensive property because it is the same whether we take a small amount of the solution or a large amount of the solution. It is defined as concentration of solute per liter of solution.

Similar to boiling point, the freezing point is an intensive property. It remains constant for a particular substance at a given pressure, regardless of the amount of the substance present. For instance, the freezing point of water is 0 degrees Celsius at sea level and doesn't change based on the quantity of water.
As all of the above option are correct, hence this is the best option.

questions.explanation: An extensive property of a system is that property which depends upon the amount of the substance or substances e.g. mass, volume, energy, entropy, Gibbs potential, enthalpy. Whereas intensive properties are those properties of a system, which are characteristic of the substance or substances present, and are independent of its mass. Temperature, pressure, density, heat capacity, boiling point etc. are intensive property.

questions.text: How many grams of butane must be burnt to heat 10 kg water from 30°C to 100°C? The heat of combustion of butane is −700 kcal/mol and the specific heat of water is 1 cal gm−1 K−1.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Q = m x c x ΔT where, Q = heat energy required m = mass of the water c = specific heat of water ΔT = change in temperature (final temperature - initial temperature) Given values, m = 10 kg = 10,000 gm (since 1 kg = 1000 gm) c = 1 cal gm-1 K-1 ΔT = 100°C - 30°C = 70°C Plugging in the values, Q = 10,000 gm x 1 cal gm-1 K-1 x 70 K Q = 700,000 cal Now, we need to convert the heat energy from calories to grams of butane. The heat of combustion of butane is given as -700 kcal/mol. To convert this to calories per gram of butane, we need to find the molar mass of butane. The molar mass of butane (C4H10) Molar mass = (4 x atomic mass of carbon) + (10 x atomic mass of hydrogen) = 4 x 12.01 g/mol + 10 x 1.01 g/mol = 48.04 g/mol Now, we can calculate the heat energy released per gram of butane, -700 kcal/mol / 48.04 g/mol ≈ -14.57 kcal/g ≈ -14.57 x 1000 cal/g ≈ -14,570 cal/g Since the heat of combustion is given as a negative value, we use the absolute value for the calculation. So, for every 14,570 cal of heat energy released, we need 1 gram of butane. Finally, the amount of butane needed to produce 700,000 cal is, 700,000 cal / 14,570 cal/g ≈ 48.04 g ≈ 48 g The correct option is A) 58 gm.

questions.text: Which of the following thermodynamic quantities is an outcome of the second law of thermodynamics?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In thermodynamics, work is defined as the energy transferred to or from a system as a result of a force acting on the system through a distance. Work is represented by the symbol W and is typically measured in joules (J). There are two types of work that can be done on a system: expansion work and non-expansion work. Expansion work occurs when a system expands or contracts against an external pressure, while non-expansion work occurs when work is done on or by a system in other ways, such as stirring or mixing. The amount of work done on a system can be calculated using the following equation: W = -PΔV where W is the work done on the system, P is the external pressure, and ΔV is the change in volume of the system. The negative sign in the equation indicates that work is done on the system when the volume decreases (i.e., pressure is positive), and work is done by the system when the volume increases (i.e., pressure is negative). Work is an important concept in thermodynamics, and it plays a central role in understanding the behavior of many physical and chemical systems.

Enthalpy is a thermodynamic property that describes the total heat content of a system at constant pressure. It is represented by the symbol H and is defined as the sum of the internal energy of the system and the product of the pressure and volume of the system: H = U + PV where U is the internal energy of the system, P is the pressure, and V is the volume. Enthalpy is often used in chemical thermodynamics to describe the heat flow in chemical reactions. For example, the enthalpy change of a reaction (ΔH) is the difference between the enthalpy of the products and the enthalpy of the reactants. If the enthalpy change is negative, the reaction releases heat (exothermic), and if it is positive, the reaction absorbs heat (endothermic).

Internal energy is the total energy contained within a system, which includes the kinetic and potential energies of all the particles that make up the system. It is a state function, which means that it depends only on the current state of the system, not on how the system got to that state. Internal energy can be changed through the transfer of heat or work into or out of the system. When heat is added to a system, the internal energy of the system increases, and when heat is removed, the internal energy decreases. Similarly, when work is done on a system, its internal energy increases, and when work is done by the system, its internal energy decreases. The change in internal energy of a system can be calculated using the First Law of Thermodynamics, which states that the change in internal energy of a system is equal to the heat added to the system minus the work done by the system: ΔU = Q - W Where ΔU is the change in internal energy, Q is the heat added to the system, and W is the work done by the system. Internal energy is an important concept in thermodynamics, and it plays a central role in understanding the behavior of many physical and chemical systems.

questions.text: Let V denote the root mean square speed of the molecules in an ideal diatomic gas at absolute temperature T. The mass of a molecule is 'm'. Neglecting vibrational energy terms, which is false?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: A molecule can have a speed greater than √2v
options.explanation: This statement is true. In a gas, the molecules have a wide range of speeds following a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution. While the most probable speed is √2v, it's entirely possible for a molecule to have a speed greater than this value due to the distribution's tail end.

This statement is true according to the kinetic theory of gases. Root mean square speed (V) of gas molecules is proportional to the square root of temperature (T). This relationship is defined by the equation V = √(3kT/m), where k is the Boltzmann constant and m is the mass of the molecule.

options.text: The average rotational kinetic energy of a molecule is mv2/4
options.explanation: This statement is false. For a diatomic molecule neglecting vibrational terms, the average rotational kinetic energy is actually 2/3 × k × T, where k is the Boltzmann constant. It's important to note that this statement incorrectly represents the average rotational kinetic energy. Hence, this option is correct.

options.text: The average kinetic energy of a molecule is 5mv2/6
options.explanation: this is the correct statement.

options.explanation: No, mud is not a conductor of heat. It is actually a good insulator of heat. Mud is a mixture of clay, sand, and water. The clay particles in mud are very small and they have a lot of surface area. This allows them to trap air, which is a good insulator of heat. The sand particles in mud also help to insulate the mud.

options.text: mud is a superconductor of heat

options.text: mud is a bad conductor of heat
options.explanation: Yes, mud houses are cooler in summer and warmer in winter because mud is a bad conductor of heat.Heat is transferred through three ways: conduction, convection, and radiation. Conduction is the transfer of heat through direct contact. Convection is the transfer of heat through the movement of fluids. Radiation is the transfer of heat through electromagnetic waves. Mud is a poor conductor of heat. This means that it does not transfer heat very well. In the summer, when the outside air is hot, the mud will not transfer the heat into the house very quickly. options.explanation: Entropy is a thermodynamic property that measures the degree of disorder or randomness in a system. The unit of entropy is Joules per Kelvin per mole (J/K·mol) or simply Joules per Kelvin (J/K) for a substance with a molar quantity of one mole.

The general gas constant, often denoted as R, is a fundamental constant in the ideal gas law equation (PV = nRT), where P is the pressure, V is the volume, n is the number of moles of gas, T is the temperature in Kelvin, and R is the gas constant. The unit of the general gas constant is Joules per Kelvin per mole (J/K·mol).

Internal energy (U) is the total energy contained within a system due to the random motion of its particles (atoms, molecules, etc.). The unit of internal energy is Joules (J) for a single molecule or atom. However, for a molar quantity (one mole) of substance, the unit is Joules per mole (J/mol).

Pressure is the force per unit area applied to a surface. The SI unit of pressure is Pascal (Pa), which is equal to one Newton per square meter (N/m²). In the context of this question, the pressure unit does not match the unit of molar specific heat.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B because the unit of molar specific heat is the same as that of the general gas constant. Both have units of Joules per Kelvin per mole (J/K·mol). This unit is used to represent the amount of energy required to raise the temperature of one mole of a substance by one Kelvin.

It is denoted as γ = Cp / Cv.

According to this option, the value of γ for this gas is 5/3. This is the correct answer. For a monoatomic gas, such as noble gases like helium (He), neon (Ne), and argon (Ar), the value of γ is 5/3. questions.text: Compressed air coming out of punctured football becomes cooler because of:

options.explanation: Isothermal expansion does not cause the air to cool. In an isothermal expansion, the temperature of the gas remains constant as it expands. This is because the expansion is done very slowly so that the gas has time to exchange heat with its surroundings.

Energy dissipation is the process of energy being lost to the surroundings. This can happen through conduction, convection, or radiation. However, energy dissipation does not necessarily cause the air to cool. In fact, if the air is hotter than its surroundings, then energy dissipation will actually cause the air to warm up.

Adiabatic expansion is a type of expansion in which no heat is exchanged with the surroundings. This means that the only way for the gas to lose energy is by doing work. When the air expands through the small hole in the football, it does work by accelerating. This work comes from the internal energy of the air, which is what causes the air to cool.
Adiabatic compression is the opposite of adiabatic expansion. In adiabatic compression, the gas is compressed and no heat is exchanged with the surroundings. This causes the air to heat up.

questions.text: Sound passes through the air under the following process:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Isobaric process refers to a process that occurs at constant pressure. In reality, sound waves involve rapid and alternating changes in pressure as they propagate, so isobaric conditions are not met during sound propagation.

Isothermal process refers to a process that occurs at a constant temperature. Sound waves involve changes in temperature due to the compression and rarefaction of air molecules, which is not consistent with an isothermal process.

Adiabatic process refers to a process where no heat is exchanged with the surroundings. This is the process that best describes the behavior of sound waves. As a sound wave travels through air, it compresses and rarefies the air molecules, leading to changes in pressure and temperature. These changes occur rapidly and without time for heat exchange, making the adiabatic process the most suitable description of sound propagation.

This option is incorrect because both isothermal and adiabatic processes are not accurate descriptions of sound propagation in air. While the adiabatic process is a better approximation, sound waves do not adhere to either isothermal or adiabatic conditions exclusively.

When a gas undergoes adiabatic expansion, it expands against a vacuum or into a space with lower pressure, and since there is no heat transfer, the gas does work on the surroundings by pushing against the external pressure. This causes the gas to lose internal energy, which is manifested as a decrease in temperature.

In an isothermal expansion, the gas expands while its temperature remains constant. This implies that the gas is in contact with a heat reservoir that keeps its temperature constant. During isothermal expansion, the gas absorbs heat from the reservoir to perform work against the external pressure, and the heat absorbed exactly balances the work done. As a result, there is no change in internal energy or temperature.

In isothermal compression, the gas is compressed while its temperature remains constant. Similar to isothermal expansion, during isothermal compression, the gas releases heat to the reservoir as it does work against the external pressure. The heat released exactly balances the work done, resulting in no change in internal energy or temperature.

questions.text: Heat neither enters nor leave a system then its temperature:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If work is not done then temperature will remain same.
Work is being done so temperature can fall.
Work is being done so temperature can rise.

If heat neither enters nor leaves a system, then its temperature may rise or may fall. This is because the temperature of a system is determined by the balance of heat entering and leaving the system, as well as the work done on the system. If there is no heat transfer, then the only way for the temperature of the system to change is if work is done on the system. For example, if a gas is compressed adiabatically (no heat transfer), then the temperature of the gas will rise. This is because the work done on the gas increases the internal energy of the gas, which is equivalent to an increase in temperature. Conversely, if a gas is expanded adiabatically, then the temperature of the gas will fall. This is because the work done on the gas decreases the internal energy of the gas, which is equivalent to a decrease in temperature.
